title: Smt. Laxmibai @ Kamalaxi vs Smt. Yallawwa And Ors on 8 August, 2023
body: Smt. Laxmibai @ Kamalaxi vs Smt. Yallawwa And Ors on 8 August, 2023 -1- NC: 2023:KHC-K:6305 RSA No. 200296 of 2018 IN THE HIGH COURT OF KARNATAKA, KALABURAGI BENCH DATED THIS THE 8TH DAY OF AUGUST, 2023 BEFORE THE HON'BLE MRS JUSTICE K S HEMALEKHA REGULAR SECOND APPEAL NO.200296 OF 2018 (PAR) BETWEEN: SMT. LAXMIBAI @ KAMALAXI W/O PRASHURAM POL @ PAWAL AGE:39 YEARS OCC:AGRICULTURE R/O :NEAR AMBA BHAVANI TEMPLE GACHINAKATTI COLONY TQ & DIST - VIJAYAPUR. ...APPELLANT (BY SRI. MAHANTESH PATIL, ADVOCATE) AND: 1. SMT. YALLAWWA W/O BALAPPA CHALLAGI AGE:61 YEARS Digitally signed OCC:AGRI by SHILPA R TENIHALLI R/O : KALAGURKI Location: HIGH COURT OF TQ: B.BAGEWADI KARNATAKA DIST:VIJAYAPUR-586101. 2. SRI.RAMESH S/O BALAPPA CHALLAGI AGE:41 YEARS, OCC:AGRI R/O SHAPUR STREET, BEHIND MSRTC DEPOT, -2- NC: 2023:KHC-K:6305 RSA No. 200296 of 2018 NEAR AMBEDKAR TEMPLE, ICHALKARNAJI, TQ & DIST.KOLHAPUR 416552 (MAHARASHTRA) 3. SRI.SURESH S/O BALAPPA CHALLAGI AGE:37 YEARS OCC:AGRICULTURE R/O : KALAGURKI TQ.: B.BAGEWADI DIST: VIJAYAPUR-586101. ...RESPONDENTS (BY SRI. SANJEEVKUMAR C. PATIL, ADVOCATE) THIS RSA IS FILED U/S. 100 OF CPC PRAYING TO SET ASIDE THE JUDGMENT AND DECREE DATED:18.06.2018 PASSED BY THE LEARNED II ADDL. DISTRICT JUDGE VIJAYAPURA IN R.A.NO.109/2017 AND CONSEQUENTLY RESTORE THE JUDGMENT AND DECREE DATED 21.07.2017 IN O.S.NO.117/2015 PASSED BY THE LEARNED III ADDL. SENIOR CIVIL JUDGE VIJAYAPURA, IN THE INTEREST OF JUSTICE AND EQUITY. THIS APPEAL COMING ON FOR ADMISSION, THIS DAY, THE COURT DELIVERED THE FOLLOWING: -3- NC: 2023:KHC-K:6305 RSA No. 200296 of 2018 JUDGMENT The present appeal by the plaintiff assailing the judgment and decree dated 18.06.2018 in RA No.109/2017 on the file of the II Additional District Judge, Vijaypura, whereby, the appeal filed by the defendants came to be allowed and directed the Trial Court to return the plaint to the plaintiff for presentation before the proper Court. 2. The parties herein are referred to as per their ranking before the Trial Court for the sake of convenience. 3. The plaintiff filed a suit for partition and separate possession claiming 1/4th share in respect of suit schedule 'A' and 'B' properties. 4. Schedule 'A' properties are: a) Land bearing Sy. No.297/1 measuring 4 acres, b) Sy. No.297/2 measuring 5 acres, c) Sy. No.297/3 measuring 7 acres and -4- NC: 2023:KHC-K:6305 RSA No. 200296 of 2018 d) Sy. No.297/4 measuring 6 acres 37 guntas situated at Kalgurki in Basavana Bagewadi Taluk and Schedule 'B' property is a land measuring 1 guntas of total area measuring 2 acres of Sy. No.107/2 of Mahalbagayat i.e., private plot No.47 situated at Vijaypura. 5. The Trial Court by its judgment and decree, partly decreed the suit of the plaintiff, holding that the plaintiff is entitled for 1/4th share in the suit schedule property bearing Sy. No.297 measuring 23 acres 37 guntas situated at Kalgurki Village as mentioned in the 'A' schedule property. The claim in respect of 'B' schedule property was rejected by the Trial Court. 6. The defendants preferred an appeal against the judgment and decree of the Trial Court in granting 1/4th share in respect of Schedule 'A' property. Before the First Appellate Court, the defendants raised a contention that the schedule 'A' property is situated at Kalgurki in -5- NC: 2023:KHC-K:6305 RSA No. 200296 of 2018 Basavana Bagewadi Taluk and schedule 'B' property is situated at Vijayapura and the plaintiff has not shown the separate valuation of land for claiming territorial jurisdiction, if the properties are lying in different places, then a separate valuation is to be shown. The defendant raised the contention that the Trial Court has given a finding that the schedule 'B' property is the self acquired property of defendant No.1. 7. According to the respondents i.e., defendants, the contention was taken before the First Appellate Court that if the schedule 'B' property was taken away, the Trial Court would have lost the territorial jurisdiction and as such, the Trial Court was barred from entertaining the suit of the plaintiff, as schedule 'A' property, is not within the territorial jurisdiction of Vijaypura Court. 8. The First Appellate Court accepted the said contention and held that the jurisdiction of the Trial Court has been taken away, since schedule 'B' property is not within the territorial jurisdiction of the Trial Court and the -6- NC: 2023:KHC-K:6305 RSA No. 200296 of 2018 Trial Court could not have passed the judgment in respect of schedule 'A' property. Aggrieved by which, the present appeal by the plaintiff. 9. Heard Sri. Mahantesh Patil, learned counsel for the appellant and Sri. Sanjeevkumar C. Patil, learned counsel for respondents. 10. The substantial question of law that arises for consideration in the present appeal is: "Whether the First Appellate Court was justified in holding that the Trial Court lacks territorial jurisdiction in the facts and circumstances of the present case?" 11. Schedule 'A' property is situated at Kalgurki and Schedule 'B' property is situated at Vijaypura, as could be seen from the plaint averments. The defendants did not raise any objections relating to the territorial jurisdiction at the first instance before the Trial Court, only before the First Appellate Court, since issue No.3 was held in favour of defendant No.1 that schedule 'B' property is the -7- NC: 2023:KHC-K:6305 RSA No. 200296 of 2018 absolute property of defendant No.1, contends that the jurisdiction of the Court in entertaining the suit in respect of 'A' schedule property would not arise, since the properties are situated at Kalgurki, the said contention has been accepted by the First Appellate Court overlooking the settled principles of law. The First Appellate Court, without looking to the provisions of Section 17 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, has directed the Trial Court to return the plaint to the plaintiff, which is not justifiable, Section 17 of CPC reads as under: "17. Suits for immovable property situate within jurisdiction of different Courts.- Where a suit is to obtain relief respecting, or compensation for wrong to, immovable property situate within the jurisdiction of different Courts, the suit may be instituted in any Court within the local limits of whose jurisdiction any portion of the property is situate : Provided that, in respect of the value of the subject-matter of the suit, the entire claim is cognizable by such Court." -8- NC: 2023:KHC-K:6305 RSA No. 200296 of 2018 The said section envisages that when the immovable property situated within the jurisdiction of different Court, the suit may be instituted in any Courts within the local limits of whose jurisdiction any portion of the property is situate. 12. The Apex Court in the case of Shivnarayan (dead) by Legal Representatives Vs. Maniklal (dead) through legal representatives and others reported in (2020) 11 SCC 629 has held that Section 17 engrafts an exception to the general rule as occurring in Section 16 CPC, as per Section 17 , the suit may be instituted in any Court within the local limits of whose jurisdiction any portion of the property is situated. The Apex Court held that the words "any portion of the property" are indicative of a fact situation where immovable property is a big chunk of land, which falls into territorial jurisdiction of two courts in which fact situation in court in whose jurisdiction any portion of property is situated can entertain the suit. The Apex Court held that the word "property" under -9- NC: 2023:KHC-K:6305 RSA No. 200296 of 2018 Section 17 may also be properties in a schedule of plaint, more than one property can be included and words "portion of the property" occurring in Section 17 have to be understood in the context of more than one property also, meaning thereby one property out of a lot of several properties can be treated as portion of the property as occurring in Section 17 . The Apex Court at paragraph Nos.33 and 34 has held as under: "33. Sections 16 and 17 CPC are part of the one statutory scheme. Section 16 contains general principle that suits are to be instituted where subject-matter is situate whereas Section 17 engrafts an exception to the general rule as occurring in Section 16 . 34. From the foregoing discussions, we arrive at the following conclusions with regard to ambit and scope of Section 17 CPC. 34.1. The word "property" occurring in Section 17 although has been used in "singular" but by virtue of Section 13 of the General Clauses Act it may also be read as "plural", i.e., "properties". 34.2. The expression "any portion of the property" can be read as portion of one or more properties situated in jurisdiction of different courts and can be - 10 - NC: 2023:KHC-K:6305 RSA No. 200296 of 2018 also read as portion of several properties situated in jurisdiction of different courts. 34.3. A suit in respect of immovable property or properties situate in jurisdiction of different courts may be instituted in any court within whose local limits of jurisdiction, any portion of the property or one or more properties may be situated. 34.4. A suit in respect to more than one property situated in jurisdiction of different courts can be instituted in a court within local limits of jurisdiction where one or more properties are situated provided suit is based on same cause of action with respect to the properties situated in jurisdiction of different courts." 13. Admittedly, schedule 'B property' is situated at Vijaypura within the territorial jurisdiction of the Trial Court. In light of the judgment of the Apex Court and the provision makes it clear that where one of the suit property is situated, that Court will have territorial jurisdiction. The First Appellate Court committed serious legal error in directing the Trial Court to return the plaint to the plaintiff for presentation before the proper Court - 11 - NC: 2023:KHC-K:6305 RSA No. 200296 of 2018 and accordingly, the point framed for consideration is answered in favour of the appellant and this Court pass the following: ORDER i. The regular second appeal filed by the plaintiff is allowed. ii. The matter is remitted back to the First Appellate Court to reconsider the matter afresh on merits, in accordance with law. iii. The parties are directed to be present before the First Appellate Court on 30.08.2023 without awaiting for further notice. iv. The First Appellate Court to reconsider the matter afresh and dispose of the appeal as expeditiously as possible. v. All contentions of the parties are kept open. Sd/- JUDGE MBM CT:NS List No.: 1 Sl No.: 59
